landscapeNote: EMC has a genomically QUIET landscape: clinical NGS of metastatic EMC found no recurrent clinically-actionable mutations (CDKN2A/2B copy loss noted), and the KIT mutation is a rare (~5%) exception. Repurposing should therefore target the NR4A3 fusion / lineage biology (angiogenesis, PPARγ, transcription/epigenetics, apoptosis) and lean on unbiased patient-derived-model drug screens, rather than expecting actionable driver mutations.
disclaimer: RESEARCH HYPOTHESES ONLY. These are mechanistic ideas and prioritization scores for investigation - not treatments, not medical advice, and not endorsed for clinical use. None of these drugs is known to work in EMC. Not for the patient-facing site. See research/hypotheses/METHODOLOGY.md.

tiers.T0-mechanistic: Mechanistic rationale only; no EMC or close-analog data.
tiers.T1-preclinical-or-analog: Preclinical signal (in vitro/in vivo/screen) or strong analogy in a related fusion-driven sarcoma.
tiers.T3-emc-clinical-evidence: Prospective or substantial clinical evidence in EMC (eligible to graduate to the patient page after clinician review).

scoring.method: Each candidate is scored 0-3 on six criteria; composite priorityScore = sum (max 18). A transparent prioritization heuristic, NOT a probability of success.

criteria.emcEvidence: Strength of EMC-specific evidence (0 mechanism-only … 3 EMC clinical).
criteria.mechanisticFit: Directness of engagement with a validated EMC driver.
criteria.availability: 0 preclinical-only … 2 investigational w/ human safety … 3 approved.
criteria.biomarker: Existence of a biomarker to select likely responders.
criteria.novelty: Degree to which it is untried in EMC.

candidates.patientPageEligible: T3 — eligible for emergingTreatments pending clinician review
candidates.enumerationProvenance: Systematic DGIdb enumeration (target-drug-matrix.json) lists these approved KIT inhibitors as alternatives for the KIT-mutant subset; choice should match the specific KIT mutation's drug sensitivity, not just KIT positivity.
candidates.mechanism: Inhibits KIT receptor tyrosine kinase signalling; exon-11 KIT mutations are the most imatinib-sensitive class in GIST.

emcVulnerability.claim: A somatic, imatinib-sensitive-class KIT exon-11 mutation occurs in a minority of EMC, and CD117/KIT is expressed in roughly half of cases.

supportingEvidence.claim: An EMC patient with a KIT exon-11 mutation (c.1669T>G) and pulmonary metastases achieved stable disease on imatinib for 3 years.
Somatic heterozygous KIT exon-11 in-frame deletion (D579del) with high KIT mRNA and receptor phosphorylation in 1/20 EMC; exon-11 mutations are the most imatinib-sensitive in GIST.
CD117(KIT) immunopositivity in 52.6% and a KIT p.E554K mutation in 2/48 EMC.

candidates.rationale: For the molecularly-selected minority with an actionable KIT exon-11 mutation, imatinib is a shelf-ready, biomarker-driven option — and a KIT-mutant EMC patient has already achieved 3-year disease stabilization on it, making this the most clinically-supported lead. Applies only to the KIT-mutant subset, not EMC generally.
candidates.keyRisks: Single case; only ~5% carry an actionable KIT mutation; CD117 expression alone does not prove KIT dependence; requires NGS selection.
openQuestions: What is the response rate to imatinib across KIT-mutant EMC beyond single cases?

candidates.mechanism: Induces PPARγ (via Krox20, C/EBPβ/α), upregulating p21/p27/p53 and driving cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis.

emcVulnerability.claim: PPARγ is an antitumour target in chondrosarcoma; inducing it arrests EMC cell growth.

supportingEvidence.claim: Zaltoprofen induced PPARγ and inhibited proliferation of EMC cells in vitro AND inhibited tumour growth (with apoptosis) in a mouse EMC model, while being well tolerated.

candidates.rationale: An approved, low-toxicity NSAID with direct in vivo anti-EMC activity through a defined mechanism — one of the strongest repurposing leads in EMC.
candidates.keyRisks: Preclinical only; PPARγ induction by an NSAID may not translate to clinical efficacy; NSAID class GI/renal/CV effects with chronic use.
openQuestions: Does zaltoprofen (or direct PPARγ agonists) show activity in EMC patients or PDX models?

candidates.drug: Regorafenib, cabozantinib, lenvatinib, nintedanib, sorafenib, axitinib, vandetanib, tivozanib, fruquintinib (approved multi-target VEGFR-TKIs)
candidates.enumerationProvenance: Systematic DGIdb target->drug enumeration (research/hypotheses/target-drug-matrix.json): each carries an 'inhibitor' interaction against EMC's VEGFR/PDGFR/KIT/RET targets and is neither catalogued nor reported as tried in EMC. Triaged OUT of this cluster: erlotinib & romiplostim (no inhibitor edge - indirect/regulatory) and rabeprazole (a PPI - biologically implausible DGIdb artifact).
candidates.mechanism: VEGFR2-centred multikinase inhibition suppressing tumour angiogenesis.

emcVulnerability.claim: Anti-angiogenic TKIs are EMC's most consistently active systemic class, and EMC expresses VEGFR/PDGFR-family kinases.

supportingEvidence.claim: Pazopanib ORR 18%, median PFS 19 months (phase 2); sunitinib ~60% partial responses.
Transcriptome profiling of metastatic EMC measured expression of sunitinib-target kinases KDR/VEGFR2, PDGFRA/B, KIT, RET, FLT1/4.
Cabozantinib (VEGF+MET TKI) was active in refractory soft-tissue sarcoma broadly (6/54 partial responses) but has not been reported specifically in EMC.

candidates.rationale: If VEGFR blockade is the most active class, other already-approved VEGFR-TKIs with deeper sarcoma data are rational, shelf-ready candidates never reported in EMC. A systematic public-database screen (DGIdb) independently surfaced this exact cluster - nintedanib, sorafenib, axitinib, vandetanib and tivozanib were among the highest target-reach approved agents untried in EMC - converting this from a hand-picked example into a reproducible shortlist.
candidates.keyRisks: Class toxicity (hypertension, hand-foot, bleeding); likely to share pazopanib's resistance; TAF15::NR4A3 tumours may respond less.
openQuestions: Does fusion partner (EWSR1 vs TAF15) or KDR expression predict response across the class?

candidates.mechanism: Direct PPARγ agonism — the same nuclear-receptor axis zaltoprofen engages indirectly.

emcVulnerability.claim: PPARγ is an antitumour target in chondrosarcoma; its activation drives differentiation/cell-cycle arrest.

supportingEvidence.claim: Zaltoprofen's anti-EMC effect was mediated by PPARγ induction, implicating PPARγ agonism as the actionable node.

candidates.rationale: If PPARγ induction drives the anti-EMC effect, a direct, approved PPARγ agonist (pioglitazone) is an obvious low-toxicity test — untried in EMC.
candidates.keyRisks: PPARγ agonism is preclinically rational but unproven in EMC; pioglitazone class warnings (fluid retention, bladder-cancer signal).
openQuestions: Does direct PPARγ agonism replicate zaltoprofen's anti-EMC activity?

candidates.mechanism: Inhibit CDK4/6-cyclin-D to restore RB-mediated cell-cycle arrest; CDKN2A loss increases dependence on CDK4/6.

emcVulnerability.claim: EMC expresses CDK4 (100% by IHC in one series) and shows CDKN2A/CDKN2B copy loss, suggesting cell-cycle/CDK4-6 dependence.

supportingEvidence.claim: CDK4 immunopositive in 100% of cases in a 31-case EMC series.
Copy losses including CDKN2A/CDKN2B observed on EMC exome analysis.

candidates.rationale: CDK4 expression plus CDKN2A/2B loss is a recognised CDK4/6-inhibitor-sensitising context; palbociclib is approved and untried in EMC.
candidates.keyRisks: Expression/copy-loss is not proven functional dependence; CDK4/6-inhibitor cytopenias; EMC's quiet genome may limit single-agent effect.
openQuestions: Is EMC RB-proficient and CDK4/6-dependent in functional assays?

candidates.mechanism: HDAC inhibition reshapes the chromatin/transcriptional state on which fusion oncoproteins depend.

emcVulnerability.claim: EMC is driven by an NR4A3 fusion transcription factor; epigenetic/transcriptional state is a candidate dependency.

supportingEvidence.claim: High-throughput screen of 221 anticancer drugs on a patient-derived EMC cell line (NCC-EMC1-C1) identified romidepsin, panobinostat and brigatinib as low-IC50 hits.

candidates.rationale: An unbiased drug screen on an EMC cell line flagged two approved HDAC inhibitors — concrete, EMC-derived preclinical support for an epigenetic strategy.
candidates.keyRisks: Single cell-line screen; HDAC-inhibitor toxicity (cytopenias, cardiac); in vitro IC50 may not translate.
openQuestions: Do HDAC inhibitors show activity in EMC PDX/in vivo models?

emcVulnerability.claim: EMC shows pan-Trk immunopositivity and NTRK2/3 transcript upregulation.

supportingEvidence.claim: Pan-Trk positive in 35.4% of EMC with NTRK2/3 upregulation by RNA sequencing — but NO NTRK gene fusion was identified.

candidates.rationale: Pan-Trk/NTRK expression raises the question of TRK inhibition, but EMC lacks NTRK fusions and expression alone rarely predicts TRK-inhibitor response — included to document the lead and its weakness.
candidates.keyRisks: Expression is not an NTRK fusion; TRK inhibitors work in fusion-positive disease, so the mechanistic rationale here is weak.
openQuestions: Is any EMC TRK-pathway-dependent in functional assays?

candidates.mechanism: Inhibits anti-apoptotic BCL-2 to lower the apoptotic threshold.

emcVulnerability.claim: Patient-derived EMC ex vivo models are sensitive to venetoclax, implying an apoptotic vulnerability.

supportingEvidence.claim: Venetoclax sensitivity was identified and validated (with doxorubicin and carfilzomib, including synergies) across two patient-derived EMC ex vivo models.

candidates.rationale: An approved BH3-mimetic with validated activity in two independent EMC models; a rational, biomarker-testable (BCL-2) repurposing lead, especially in combination.
candidates.keyRisks: Ex vivo only; BCL-2 dependence not yet confirmed in vivo; tumour-lysis/cytopenia risks.
openQuestions: Does venetoclax (alone or with anthracycline) show activity in EMC in vivo?

candidates.mechanism: ALK/ROS1 and multikinase inhibition; mechanism of any EMC effect is undefined (possible off-target multikinase activity).

emcVulnerability.claim: Empirical sensitivity in an EMC cell-line drug screen, mechanism not yet defined.

supportingEvidence.claim: Low IC50 in the NCC-EMC1-C1 patient-derived EMC cell-line screen.

candidates.rationale: A hypothesis-generating screen hit worth mechanistic follow-up; included for completeness with the caveat that its EMC mechanism is unknown.
candidates.keyRisks: Single-cell-line screen; no mechanistic rationale; ALK/ROS1 not implicated in EMC.
openQuestions: What target mediates brigatinib's effect in EMC cells?

candidates.mechanism: Irreversible proteasome inhibition causing proteotoxic stress and apoptosis.

emcVulnerability.claim: Patient-derived EMC ex vivo models are sensitive to carfilzomib.

supportingEvidence.claim: Carfilzomib sensitivity identified and validated across two patient-derived EMC ex vivo models, including synergistic combinations.

candidates.rationale: Approved proteasome inhibitor with validated EMC ex vivo activity; worth mechanistic follow-up and combination testing.
candidates.keyRisks: Ex vivo only; mechanism of EMC sensitivity undefined; cardiac/renal toxicity.
openQuestions: What drives proteasome-inhibitor sensitivity in EMC, and does it hold in vivo?

candidates.mechanism: Pair standard anthracycline cytotoxicity with an apoptotic (BCL-2) or proteotoxic (proteasome) sensitiser identified as synergistic in EMC models.

emcVulnerability.claim: EMC ex vivo models showed drug synergies between doxorubicin and venetoclax/carfilzomib.

supportingEvidence.claim: Synergistic combinations (doxorubicin with venetoclax/carfilzomib) were identified by functional screening in patient-derived EMC models.

candidates.rationale: EMC is anthracycline-treated already; adding a screen-validated synergistic partner is a low-barrier way to improve on a current backbone.
candidates.keyRisks: Ex vivo synergy may not translate; overlapping toxicities; combination trials are harder in an ultra-rare disease.
openQuestions: Does anthracycline + venetoclax/carfilzomib improve response over anthracycline alone in EMC models?

candidates.drug: NR4A3/NOR1-directed modulators (e.g. prostaglandin-A2-related compounds, NR4A ligands)
candidates.mechanism: Pharmacologically modulate the NR4A3 (NOR1) nuclear receptor that, as the EWSR1::NR4A3 fusion, drives EMC.

emcVulnerability.claim: The EWSR1::NR4A3 fusion transcription factor is EMC's defining oncogenic driver, and the NOR1 ligand-binding domain is pharmacologically engageable despite 'orphan' status.

supportingEvidence.claim: Prostaglandin A2 binds the NOR1 ligand-binding domain and transactivates it, indicating NOR1 is druggable.
NR4A-family receptors regulate proliferation/apoptosis and are an emerging therapeutic target class.

candidates.rationale: The highest-value but hardest target: hit the fusion's DNA-binding receptor directly. Mechanistically central; pharmacologically immature.
candidates.keyRisks: No clinical-grade NR4A3 drug exists; whether to agonise or antagonise the fusion is unclear; selectivity within the NR4A family.
openQuestions: Can a drug-like molecule modulate EWSR1::NR4A3 transcriptional output and kill EMC cells?

candidates.mechanism: Inhibit transcriptional co-regulators (BRD4, CDK7/9) that fusion oncoproteins depend on.

emcVulnerability.claim: EMC's NR4A3 fusion is a chimeric transcription factor that activates target genes via chromatin modification — a candidate transcriptional/chromatin dependency.

supportingEvidence.claim: The EMC fusion oncoprotein (TFG::NR4A3) drives transcriptional activation of target genes via chromatin modification of their promoters, indicating a chromatin/transcription dependency.

candidates.rationale: Targets the fusion's transcriptional/chromatin addiction (an EMC-native mechanism); related EWSR1-fusion sarcomas such as Ewing are reportedly BET/CDK-sensitive, though that analogy is unconfirmed for EMC. Investigational agents, no EMC drug data yet.
candidates.keyRisks: Narrow therapeutic window/toxicity; the Ewing analogy may not transfer; no EMC data.
openQuestions: Is EWSR1::NR4A3 transcriptionally dependent on BRD4 or CDK7/9 in EMC models?

candidates.drug: mRNA-LNP vaccine + PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor (± anti-angiogenic TKI)
candidates.mechanism: mRNA-LNP innate-immune agonism (type-I IFN) to convert a cold tumour hot, then checkpoint blockade to license a T-cell response.

emcVulnerability.claim: EMC is immunologically quiet, with checkpoint inhibitors not systematically active and only isolated immune responses reported.

supportingEvidence.claim: mRNA–lipid-nanoparticle vaccines are intrinsically immunostimulatory, driving type-I interferon and innate-immune activation (TLR/RIG-I/MDA5) without a separate adjuvant.

In resected high-risk melanoma, adding an individualised neoantigen mRNA vaccine to pembrolizumab improved recurrence-free survival vs pembrolizumab alone (HR 0.56) in a randomised phase 2b trial (KEYNOTE-942).

candidates.rationale: Address the cold-microenvironment problem with a readily available immune stimulant plus checkpoint blockade; the EWSR1::NR4A3 junction is a theoretical neoantigen.
candidates.keyRisks: Sarcoma immunotherapy generally underperforms; low neoantigen load; immune toxicity; no EMC data.
openQuestions: Does EMC have enough T-cell infiltration to reinvigorate?
